approach: CLEARANCE GRANTED LIVE by the owner in-session ('It is ok to unfreeze to get the behavior correctly'), scoped to the plugin/bundle node. The node is REWRITTEN, not edited: universal-plugin is a PUBLISHED npm tool that must serve any release mechanism (semantic-release included), and the frozen suite baked THIS repo's changesets policy into a general-purpose contract. Narrowed/deleted frozen scenarios include 'pins to the workspace version even when a registry would resolve differently', 'resolves from the workspace with no network access', 'a pin for a package with no workspace entry is left untouched', and the broken-workspace-entry skip. Offline support explicitly dropped by the owner.

New model: bundle retires; plugin deps ls|up replaces it, package-manager-shaped (pnpm up / pnpm ls). The inWorkspace axis is DELETED entirely - ownership was never the real selector; the declared RANGE is. Versions resolve from a registry (--registry, default npmjs.org). Named deps rather than packages: packages/* already means the workspace glob in this repo, so 'plugin packages ls' would read as listing workspace members.

Write semantics settled with the owner: bare npx pkg -> npx pkg@x.y.z (exact, the security default); up pkg@^2.0.0 writes the range through (owner chose pnpm-faithful over always-exact); --exact/-E resolves a range to exact (established npm/yarn name over --resolve); --latest ignores declared ranges. No lockfile exists, so a written-through range means npx resolves at RUN time - which is what makes ls's 'what does this resolve to' view load-bearing.

Release flow inverts: pinning moves AFTER changeset publish as its own CI step over the network (root package.json 'version' script + .github/workflows/release.yml both change), replacing 'changeset version && pnpm run bundle'.

SUBSUMES github-315 (bundle silently skips pin rewriting from the plugin dir). The reported defect is real and was reproduced (--root . from a plugin dir never walks up: path.dirname('.') === '.' terminates findMonorepoRoot on iteration one, so the workspace map comes back empty and a local package is misfiled as external, exit 0). Its fix is DELETION - the workspace discovery that carries the bug is removed - so no cwd fix is landed. Both of the issue's own hypotheses were refuted before scoping: its suspected cause (walk-up missing) is false, the walk-up exists and is defeated by a relative path; its suggested signal rule (skip count == candidate count -> nonzero exit) CONTRADICTS two frozen scenarios and would have fired the Conflict floor.
Preserved: the pin-exempt doc-example ignore (upgrade-universal-plugin's illustration strings) must survive the rewrite unchanged.

Reversible feature branch; hard floors still stop. Open at spec draft: .plugin/pins.json semantics under the new model; whether deps outdated splits from ls; the plugin/build sibling's framing once the dev-vs-release axis dissolves.

summary: The spec-gate edit-class guard fails GREEN when a suite is renamed in the same change as a rewrite: classify-edit-class classifies per path, so a renamed .feature has no history at the base and every scenario reads as 'added' -> ADDITIVE, the class that self-clears and needs no re-open. Measured on this CR: the frozen bundle.feature carried 24 scenarios; rewriting it in place classifies NARROWING (0 added / 1 modified / 22 removed), but the identical deletion with a git mv to deps/deps.feature classifies ADDITIVE (40 added / 0 removed). The rename alone flips the verdict. git's own --find-renames cannot rescue it (a full rewrite leaves no similarity: bundle.feature reads as a pure 183-line delete; the unchanged sibling README.md IS detected as a rename, proving detection ran and found no match). Contradicts the completion claim that the structural classifier is what makes 'never narrow a frozen scenario without a ratified re-open' mechanically enforceable -- a Clearance-class change disguises itself as additive by renaming. Only the owner's live grant caught it on this CR. Secondary: classify-edit-class returns EGIT/UNCLASSIFIABLE for a deleted path (spawnSync ENOENT, apparently a cwd derived from the now-missing dirname), so pointing it at the OLD path is not a workaround.
Delegate plugin hook init to universal-plugin (owner-identified during the explore grill; out of scope here).

The hook-npx-pin ledger's 'npx pin dormant until legion-publish' premise is stale -- cyberlegion is published (npm 0.1.0). Related: the pin-init-skill pinning path has never executed on main (the per-plugin version map exists only on the pending release PR), so verify the read-map -> --pin -> hook relay end-to-end once this CR lands.

The pin-exempt doc-example protection does NOT protect the one real skill it exists for, and the corruption is already sitting in the pending release PR. The cyberspace upgrade-universal-plugin skill carries NO pin-exempt marker in its frontmatter (only name + description), so isPinExempt returns false and every version string in it is in scope. PROVEN by a real run against the corpus (reverted immediately): bundle rewrites 3 files, turning 'Found npx universal-plugin@1.2.3 in 4 files' into '@0.2.1', 'Replace all with npx universal-plugin@1.5.0? (y/n)' into '@0.2.1 (y/n)' (the pin regex also eats the trailing '?'), and the skill's core instruction into 'replace every npx universal-plugin@0.2.1 with npx universal-plugin@0.2.1' -- a self-defeating no-op. The skill's description frontmatter, which a router matches on, is corrupted too. This is NOT hypothetical: the same corruption is already committed in a6cc6cc4 on origin/changeset-release/main, one merge from shipping. Contradicts a completion claim in BOTH the retired bundle spec and this CR's new deps README, each of which asserts the upgrade-universal-plugin meta-skill IS marked pin-exempt. The frozen scenario passes only against a synthetic fixture that carries the marker; nothing ever checked the real corpus. Fix is one line (add metadata.pin-exempt: true to the skill) but it lands in a THIRD project (cyberspace) and the release PR needs the corrupted files reverted before merge.

cause: The THESIS in this shard's leash line is REFUTED and must not be read as the model. It says 'the skills are the manifest' -- that any npx <pkg> reference a skill carries IS a dependency declaration -- and that 'ownership was never the real selector; the declared RANGE is'. Both are false. Proven against the real corpus and the live registry by the round-5 cold spec-judge and re-verified independently: (1) `npx skills add cyberuni/cyberplace --skill <x>` appears in 29 FILES across nearly every plugin as the standard bootstrap idiom for the UNRELATED `skills` CLI, and `skills` is a real published package (npm 1.5.19) -- a bare `deps up` would rewrite every one to `npx skills@1.5.19 add`, PINNING an installer whose entire purpose is to always fetch latest: the exact inversion of the node's security thesis. (2) The literal English prose 'shipping an npx dependency' appears in 3 governance documents (sdd-spec-judge.md, aced-spec-validator.md, aced-scenario-writer.md), and `dependency` is a real published package (npm 0.0.1) -- bare `deps up` would rewrite the SENTENCE into `npx dependency@0.0.1`. `npx` followed by a word is not a declaration; sometimes it is English, sometimes it is a different tool's command. The information needed to tell them apart is NOT IN THE STRING, so no classifier over that string can work -- which is why five consecutive judge rounds each found a new defect at a new point in the input space (R1 convergence contradiction, R2 @latest-isn't-semver, R3 @* is a truthy range, R4 satisfies('latest') is false + the unspecified extraction boundary, R5 the premise itself). Sharpening the classifier was refining an answer to the wrong question.

approach: DIRECTION CHANGE ratified live by the owner after the round-5 Oracle failure: an ALLOWLIST replaces prose classification as the selector. .plugin/deps.json declares WHICH packages are managed; a name not on the list is invisible -- `npx skills add` and the prose 'npx dependency' are never seen, because nothing looks for them. The false-positive class does not shrink, it stops existing. Prose is still scanned, but only for names already known.

This is a NARROW version of a model the owner previously rejected, and the difference is why it is now acceptable: the earlier proposal ALSO moved ranges into deps.json, killing prose ranges and --exact, and the owner said 'no, that's not what I meant'. Only the NAME LIST moves. Versions stay in prose, ranges stay supported (npx accepts them natively -- measured: cyberlegion@^0.1.0 resolves to 0.1.0), --exact keeps its job, ls still reads the lock. New evidence (29 files, two registry-real false positives), not a re-litigation.

Owner also scoped IN: `deps add` / `deps remove` (the full CRUD -- how a name gets on the list), and a DETECTION command that surfaces candidate package names across a plugin's skills so the allowlist is built from evidence rather than memory. Detection is the honest answer to the bootstrapping question the judge raised twice (the old pin-exempt marker was never applied to the one skill it existed for; an ignore/allowlist entry nobody seeds fails the same way).

SURVIVES the direction change: the extraction boundary (a reference ends at whitespace or a Markdown/sentence delimiter; the delimiter is never part of the spec) and the placeholder rule (npx cyberlegion@<version> -- cyberlegion IS managed, so <version> must still read as prose). Both still needed, but their blast radius collapses to managed names only.
Scope CUT by the owner earlier this round and still cut: git specs, tarball URLs, file: paths and npm: aliases are out of scope -- npx's documented surface is <pkg>[@<version>].
Hard floors still stop. The suite is NOT re-judged against this model yet; the last verdict (round 5) is ALIGNED false on the model this replaces.

ROUND-6 RULING, settled live by the owner: a version spec in the prose is a CONSTRAINT, not a cached resolution. Bare `deps up` resolves WITHIN what the prose declares and never crosses it -- a range keeps its operator and only the lock moves; an EXACT spec is a PIN that bare `up` will not move at all. Moving a declared spec is an explicit act: name a spec (`up pkg@^2.0.0`) or pass `--latest`. Only a reference with NO spec (`npx pkg`) gets a version written by bare `up`, which is the security default. Consequence the owner accepted: after the corpus sweep every reference is exact, so bare `up` becomes a lock-refresh only and bumping is always explicit. Upside: bare `up` can never silently cross a major -- preserving the retired bundle node's no-`--allow-major` stance without a flag.

WHY this reading and not the other: the owner's own grill note -- '`up` refreshes the lock for every dep always, EVEN WHERE THE MANIFEST DOESN'T MOVE' -- only parses if the prose sometimes does not move under `up`. The rejected reading (prose = a cache of the last resolution, bare `up` rewrites everything to newest exact) makes that note incoherent and lets bare `up` cross a major with no flag. The settled write-semantics table was not wrong, it was AMBIGUOUS: its `deps up` (bare) row is keyed on the INVOCATION but its example (`npx pkg` -> `npx pkg@x.y.z`) is the bare-REFERENCE case. Same rule, under-specified. Not a re-litigation of the table.

Round-6 cold spec-judge verdict was ALIGNED false (oracle PASS, architect PASS, BUILDER FAIL): the README stated both readings in two sections and no scenario discriminated them -- the everyday `deps up` path against an already-versioned reference (i.e. every package after its first `up`) was unspecified. Judge was correct; the defect was real.

SECOND-ORDER effect of the ruling, found by re-running the discrimination test rather than by the judge: because an exact spec no longer moves under bare `up`, several scenarios that started from exact prose stopped testing their own rule. Worst case the `ignore` scenarios -- prose `npx universal-plugin@1.2.3` is a pin, so bare `up` leaves it alone WITH OR WITHOUT `ignore`, i.e. they passed with the rule DELETED. They now drive with `--latest`, which crosses the pin, so `ignore` is the only thing carrying them. Same fix applied to the extraction-boundary and all-or-nothing scenarios.

Two coverage gaps the judge raised, now closed as spec: (1) ONE SPEC PER MANAGED PACKAGE -- two skills declaring different specs for one package is an error (`up` exits 1, `ls` reports `divergent`), because the lock records one resolution per package and the cyberlegion-plugin init relay asks it 'what version shipped' expecting ONE answer; divergence would silently break that relay. (2) a managed name with NO prose reference is `unused` -- `ls` reports it, `up` records nothing; the honest inverse of `scan` (references with no entry vs an entry with no references).
Suite 53 -> 61 scenarios. Not yet re-judged at time of writing: round-7 cold judge dispatched.

summary: The root release wiring still invokes the verb this CR deletes, and NOTHING mechanical will fail until it does. VERIFIED against the repo: root package.json `version` = 'changeset version && pnpm run bundle', and `bundle` = six literal `universal-plugin plugin bundle --root plugins/<x>` invocations (aced, cyberfleet, cyberlegion, cyberspace, quill, sdd). The deps spec node no longer mentions `bundle` anywhere, and src/bundle/{bundle.ts,cli.ts} is still registered live in src/cli.ts. So the moment the impl gate removes `plugin bundle`, `pnpm run version` breaks at release time -- the least observable moment. This is IN SCOPE for this CR (the plan's deliver todo says 'rewire release flow') but is a NON-GOAL of the deps node itself (a consuming repo's CI wiring is not the command's behavior), so it can fall between the two. Sequencing constraint for deliver: retiring `plugin bundle` and rewiring package.json + .github/workflows/release.yml are ATOMIC -- the pinning step must also move AFTER `changeset publish` and pass explicit pkg@version pairs, because under the new model versions resolve from a registry and are not knowable before publish. Caught by the round-7 cold spec-judge reading package.json rather than the prose; an earlier draft of the deps README asserted this rewiring as already-present fact.

A stray reference to the retired `universal-plugin plugin bundle` verb survives outside this CR's touched nodes, at .agents/specs/sdd/design/gherkin-cli-dependency.md:26. Not in the deps node's blast radius and not gating this CR; sweep once `plugin deps` ships so the corpus carries one verb name. Surfaced by the round-7 spec-judge as an architect-lens observation.

cause: ROUNDS 6-8 were ONE defect found three times, not three defects, and the CR's own fix loop was what kept re-finding it. Diagnosis after tripping the plan's stop rule at R8: the node never stated WHICH REFERENCES PARTICIPATE in the spec/divergence/lock computation. It defined participation NEGATIVELY and PIECEMEAL across four separate places -- 'a placeholder is invisible to up', 'an ignored path is never rewritten', 'a bare reference declares nothing' -- so each cold judge picked a different PAIR of rules and asked 'does this one count?', and each answer needed a new rule. R6: bare-vs-already-versioned. R7: bare-vs-declared (a hole in R6's own fix). R8: ignored-vs-declared, placeholder-vs-declared, 3+ references (all holes in R6/R7's fixes). The term 'declaration' was itself overloaded four ways -- README:69 called a bare reference 'a declaration with no constraint' while README:129 said a bare reference 'declares nothing', a direct self-contradiction NO judge named across three rounds.
correction: The constraint 'one spec per managed package' is CORRECT and stands: the lock records one resolution per package because the cyberlegion-plugin init relay asks it 'what version shipped' expecting ONE answer, and two versions of one CLI inside one plugin is an authoring error, not a use case. What was wrong was the SPECIFICATION METHOD, not the rule. Replaced the scattered negatives with ONE total, positively-stated, first-match-wins classification table (1 ignored -> 2 placeholder -> 3 bare -> 4 declaration; only row 4 declares). Every other rule now READS OFF it and decides nothing on its own: a package's spec = what its declarations agree on; a bare reference adopts it; ignored/placeholder never count. ls's status precedence is likewise stated as a total rule (divergent > unused > ignored > placeholder > stale > unchanged). This makes the interaction class the judges kept probing UNREACHABLE rather than merely enumerated -- there is no pair left to ask about, because participation is decided in exactly one place.
evidence: Precedence matters and is now explicit: `ignore` is evaluated FIRST, on the path, so an illustration string can never perturb a real dependency -- which would have re-introduced this node's own false-positive class through a different door (an ignored `npx universal-plugin@1.2.3` blocking `up` on the real universal-plugin dependency as `divergent`). Also recorded: the extraction boundary means `@1.5.0?` extracts as `1.5.0`, a VALID spec, so row 2 (form) does NOT protect it and row 1 (`ignore`, a path escape) must -- this is exactly the real upgrade-universal-plugin corruption in followup #5, and it is why `ignore` is a path escape rather than a form rule. 64 -> 71 scenarios covering every closed-form interaction plus two must-survive controls.
lesson: A rule stated by its EXCEPTIONS invites one judge round per exception pair. State the discriminator ONCE, positively, as a closed form -- then new cases are decided by reading the table rather than by adding a rule.

cause: Followup #5 in this shard asserts that an unseeded `ignore` 'fails identically' to the unwired `pin-exempt` marker -- i.e. that the allowlist model reproduces the same silent-corruption bug under a new name. MEASURED against the real corpus under the closed form: FALSE. The two failure modes are opposites.
correction: Ran the node's closed-form classification over every `npx <name>` occurrence in all six real plugin projects (scoped per plugin project, which is the rule's scope). Result for the one genuinely dangerous case -- `universal-plugin` in plugins/cyberspace: EVERY declared spec it carries (`@1.2.3` at upgrade-universal-plugin/SKILL.md:46 and `@1.5.0` at :51) lives INSIDE the illustration file that must be ignored. Outside that file the package has only BARE references (skills/universal-plugin/SKILL.md:17, skills/write-vendor-config/SKILL.md:48) and placeholders (skills/init/SKILL.md:51 `@<exact>`). So: WITH `ignore` seeded, row 1 removes both declarations, the package has no spec, and its bare references pin exact -- which independently reproduces the plan's measured pre-change baseline of 'cyberspace 2 pinned'. WITHOUT `ignore` seeded, those two differing declarations make the package `divergent`: `up` EXITS 1 naming universal-plugin and writes nothing. The old model's identical mistake (forgetting pin-exempt) SILENTLY CORRUPTED the file and is already committed to origin/changeset-release/main at a6cc6cc4. The unseeded-escape failure mode therefore inverted from silent-corruption to fail-loud. Do not carry followup #5's 'fails identically' reasoning forward; its OBSERVATION (the marker was never applied, the corruption is real and queued) stands and is still to be fixed at deliver.
evidence: Two further measured facts. (1) The constraint model gives a SECOND, independent layer here even if divergence never fired: `@1.2.3` is an exact spec, hence a pin, so bare `up` would not move it regardless -- only `--latest` crosses it, which is what the `ignore` scenarios now drive with. (2) The corpus found an input-space point THREE cold judge rounds did not: the real divergence is two differing declarations inside ONE FILE, and every divergence scenario in the suite used two separate files. Now frozen as 'two declarations disagreeing inside one file is divergent' plus its ignored-control twin, both transcribed from the real strings at upgrade-universal-plugin/SKILL.md:46,51 rather than invented.
lesson: Constructed fixtures are blind to the real corpus. Running the rule over the actual corpus it will police found both a refuted premise and an uncovered input-space point that adversarial judges missed.

approach: SIMPLIFICATION ruling, settled live by the owner after the round-9 dist-tag finding: STOP trying to classify the open world of npx spec strings. Support a small, CLOSED set of five reference forms and IGNORE everything else. This supersedes the round-8 'closed form' classification table (ignored/placeholder/bare/declaration with divergence machinery) -- that was still an attempt to give every possible string a meaning.

The five recognized forms of `npx <managed-name>`: (1) PROSE, bare with no @ (`npx cyberlegion`) -> WARNED, never rewritten, surfaced so a forgotten pin is visible; (2) PLACEHOLDER `@<version>` -> CONVERTED to exact by `up` (this is the recommended authoring seed form -- an author writes @<version> and `up` fills in the resolved version); (3) EXACT `@x` -> a pin, bare `up` leaves it, `--latest` moves it; (4) TILDE `@~x` -> range, resolved within, prose stays; (5) CARET `@^x` -> range, resolved within, prose stays. In 3/4/5 x is major | major.minor | major.minor.patch.

ANYTHING ELSE is IGNORED -- not rewritten, not an error, not counted. Dist-tags (@latest, @next), comparator ranges (@>=1, @*), git/tarball/npm:/file: specs, and any other string are simply not one of the five. This fixes the round-9 dist-tag finding BY CONSTRUCTION: the round-8 model classified by a NEGATIVE test ('spec is not valid semver' -> placeholder), which silently swallowed @latest as documentation; a positive, closed enumeration cannot misclassify -- an unrecognized form is ignored, never mistaken for a placeholder.

DELETED from the prior model: (a) the adopt-onto-bare rule -- prose is now warned-only and never auto-pinned, which dissolves the round-7 finding at its root since bare references no longer participate in the constraint at all; (b) placeholder-as-invisible -- placeholder now CONVERTS to exact, the exact opposite of 'never touched', because it is the authoring seed; (c) the six-status ls precedence -- now ok/stale/unpinned/unused/divergent, with bare prose surfaced as a separate warning count rather than a status; (d) the --exact flag -- redundant now that placeholder->exact is the exact-seeding path.

SURVIVES: the allowlist selector (unchanged), the lock (one resolution per package), `ignore` as a PATH escape evaluated first (still needed for the one real illustration file, upgrade-universal-plugin, whose @1.2.3/@1.5.0 are exact forms `up` would otherwise pin), and divergence as a SIMPLE guard -- two exact/tilde/caret references with DIFFERENT specs -> `up` exits 1. Prose and placeholders carry no constraint and never diverge. Suite pruned 73 -> 60 scenarios.
NOT yet re-judged against this simplified model at time of writing: round-10 cold spec-judge dispatched. The last recorded verdict (round 9) is ALIGNED false on the superseded closed-form model.

cause: The simplification leash entry (this shard, the five-forms leash) listed 'the lock (one resolution per package)' among what SURVIVES. That is now RETRACTED by an owner cut: deps.json records NO versions at all.
correction: The `resolved` field / lock is DROPPED. deps.json is only { $schema, dependencies: [names], ignore: [paths] } -- `dependencies` is a plain array of managed NAMES (owner chose `dependencies` over `packages`: it matches the deps.json filename and the `deps` command, whereas `packages` re-collides with the packages/* workspace glob, the same reason the verb is `deps` not `packages`). Proof the lock was redundant: after `up`, 4 of the 5 forms carry their version IN THE PROSE (placeholder->exact, exact stays, and prose/bare has none to record); only a range's snapshot was unique to the lock, and a range in prose MEANS 're-resolve at run time', so snapshotting it contradicts the author's explicit choice and no coherent consumer reads it. `ls stale` is gone with the lock (nothing to be stale against); statuses are now pinned/unpinned/unused/divergent + a bare-prose warning count. `up` writes SKILL FILES ONLY; deps.json changes only via add/remove.
evidence: Divergence and placeholder-adopt SURVIVE, re-grounded: their old justification was 'the lock holds one answer for the init relay'; that is gone, so they now stand on plain AUTHORING HYGIENE -- two different versions of one CLI in one plugin is a mistake worth stopping. The init relay is DECOUPLED from deps.json entirely (owner: 'init-cyberlegion will not need it; dependency management at develop time is the way to go'): `deps up` converts the init skill's OWN `npx cyberlegion@<version>` placeholder to a concrete exact pin at develop time, so init reads the version from its own already-pinned invocation and falls back to unpinned when still a placeholder -- the fallback contract preserved term-for-term, now keyed on the skill's own invocation rather than a deps.json record. This re-touches the Clearance'd init node's 3 scenarios again (collapsed to 3: the 'range never read as shipped' scenario is moot -- init reads an exact pin, never a range). Suite 60 -> 58.
